3: The Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) of the United Na- tions reported a 75% increase in the number of farmed animals globally between 1990 and 2014. At present, there are about 70 billion farmed animals and the number is expected to double by 2050 [1]. One of the main reasons is the higher demand for food and pet as human population increases [2], which corresponds to a spike in animal waste production. Traditional means of managing manure for fertilisers has proven to be inefficient as only 25% of these manures are being properly utilised while the rest are left to decompose in open space [3]. This has led to pollution of air quality from the emissions of NH3, H2S and greenhouse gases such as CH4 and NO [4], depletion of water quality due to runoffs [5], and the

transmission of disease and air-borne pathogens [6]. These reasons prompt the need to identify effective manure management methods. One of the effective ways is to utilise animal manure as biowaste source for valorisation into higher value bio-products.

Various thermochemical conversion processes, such as carbon- isation, combustion, pyrolysis, hydrothermal liquefaction and gasification, have been subjected to investigation in the valorisation of manures from cow [7], swine [8], poultry [9] and horse [10]. Among the thermochemical conversion processes, pyrolysis is known to be able to produce biochar, bio-oil and gaseous products while emitting lower gaseous pollutants [11]. The use of microwave to induce the pyrolysis process was shown to be more advanta- geous than conventional pyrolysis with external heating. Microwave-assisted pyrolysis is capable of inducing effective volumetric heating as the radiation from the electromagnetic waves can penetrate the feedstock. This overcomes the problem of uneven heating and reduces overall energy consumption. Besides, microwave heating enables higher heating rate and selectivity in improving certain product yields [12,13].

Horse manure has been reported in previous work to be low in ash and high in lignin compositions, making it a potential feedstock to be upgraded into solid fuel and bio-oil with high phenolic con- tent [14]. In addition, horse manure is naturally low in moisture and high in solid content as compared to dairy manure, thus, making it easier for processing and transporting [15]. Globally, the number of horses estimated is around 58 million [16]. An average 1000-pound horse can produce approximately 17 kg of manure daily [17]. Such substantial amount of manure waste can be reused as a sustainable feedstock for pyrolysis, with the dual aims of treating the waste and to derive useful end products. Pyrolysis process is dependent on the operating parameters, such as temperature [18], carrier gas flow rate [19] and microwave receptor proportion [20]. To fully capture the maximum yield of pyrolytic products, process optimisation is required. To the best of author's knowledge, there has yet to be any optimisation study conducted on the pyrolytic end products of horse manure. Identification of the optimum conditions is impor- tant to maximise the values of the derivatives [21] and to enable the evaluation of the environmental impact brought by the waste management pyrolysis plant.

The purpose of implementing biomass waste-to-energy tech- nology is to reduce waste volume dump to landfill, and use the waste as carbon-neutral feedstock for further processing into useful product to reduce environmental impact [22]. The overall envi- ronmental impact could be detrimental to the ecosystem if the processing method has high global warming potential (GWP) or acidification potential (AP). Thus, a life cycle assessment (LCA) is vital in assessing the overall environmental impacts and the sus- tainability of a biomass waste-to-energy processing plant. It has been reported that the pyrolysis of 1 kg municipal solid waste (MSW) generates a total energy of 5.5 MJ, of which 2.7 MJ is derived from H2 gas and bio-char yield [23]. However, the transportation process has also been found to have a significant impact on eutrophication potential (EP), while electricity usage for bio-oil production could cause ozone depletion potential (ODP) and terrestrial ecotoxicity potential (TETP). Nevertheless, fast pyrolysis of MSW has lower environmental impact when compared with other waste management processes, such as anaerobic digestion, incineration and landfilling. The processing of swine and dairy manure through pyrolysis and combustion has also been investi- gated through LCA to evaluate its environmental impact, energy production and economics [24]. It is determined that combustion of manure releases significant amount of greenhouse gases. However, it generates higher energy output and has better return of eco- nomic investment when compared with pyrolysis. Therefore, microwave-assisted pyrolysis presents an alternative solution to

conventional pyrolysis and direct combustion method, which can be scaled to industrial level for manure processing. To date, there are microwave technology specialists that manufacture industrial- scale microwave-assisted pyrolysis plant [25].

The present study aims to statistically analyse the relationship between pyrolysis factors and all the three products yield (biochar, bio-oil and biogas). Three models in predicting the pyrolytic products based on operating parameters is then established. Opti- misation is subsequently conducted by considering all three end products as each product yield varies with operating parameters. The optimised case is selected as the basis for LCA, which comprises of operating parameters, products yield and characteristics. The process energy consumption, operation cost and environmental impact are evaluated based on horse manure cradle-to-gate pro- cess. The environmental impacts such as GWP, EP, POCP, human toxicity potential (HTP) and AP are evaluated and compared with literature results to further assess the potential of microwave- induced pyrolysis.

However, the transportation process has also been found to have a significant impact on eutrophication potential (EP), while electricity usage for bio-oil production could cause ozone depletion potential (ODP) and terrestrial ecotoxicity potential (TETP). Nevertheless, fast pyrolysis of MSW has lower environmental impact when compared with other waste management processes, such as anaerobic digestion, incineration and landfilling. The processing of swine and dairy manure through pyrolysis and combustion has also been investi- gated through LCA to evaluate its environmental impact, energy production and economics [24]. It is determined that combustion of manure releases significant amount of greenhouse gases. However, it generates higher energy output and has better return of eco- nomic investment when compared with pyrolysis. Therefore, microwave-assisted pyrolysis presents an alternative solution to

conventional pyrolysis and direct combustion method, which can be scaled to industrial level for manure processing. To date, there are microwave technology specialists that manufacture industrial- scale microwave-assisted pyrolysis plant [25].

The present study aims to statistically analyse the relationship between pyrolysis factors and all the three products yield (biochar, bio-oil and biogas). Three models in predicting the pyrolytic products based on operating parameters is then established. Opti- misation is subsequently conducted by considering all three end products as each product yield varies with operating parameters. The optimised case is selected as the basis for LCA, which comprises of operating parameters, products yield and characteristics. The process energy consumption, operation cost and environmental impact are evaluated based on horse manure cradle-to-gate pro- cess. The environmental impacts such as GWP, EP, POCP, human toxicity potential (HTP) and AP are evaluated and compared with literature results to further assess the potential of microwave- induced pyrolysis.
8: The microwave pyrolysis reactor used in the present study is shown in Fig. 1. Horse manure collected from a horse stable in Universiti Teknologi Malaysia was used as the feedstock, while carbonaceous activated carbon derived from coconut shell was used as the catalyst in the present work. Results on the physi- ochemical properties of the feedstock and catalyst have been pre- sented in the authors' previous work [14]. The feedstock was placed within a quartz reactor inside the microwave cavity. Volatiles evolved during pyrolysis were then convected by the flowing N2 gas into a serially-connected condenser cooled to 9 C, whereas gaseous products were collected after passing through a bed of silica gel. The pyrolytic products of biochar, bio-oil, water and coke were subsequently measured through gravimetric means while gaseous products were derived using mass balance equation. Experimental feedstock was prepared by manually mixing the horse manure with activated carbon at mass ratio of 2:1, 1:1 and 1:2.
9: Characterisation of the end products in solid, liquid and gaseous states produced from the pyrolysis process was performed. The specific surface area (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET)) and pore volume of biochar were analysed using test methods in accordance to ASTM D3633 and ASTM D4222, respectively, via a surface ana- lyser (Thermo Scientific Surfer). Detailed characterisation proced- ure and sample preparation can be found in authors' previous work [26]. The elemental composition of bio-oil (in dry basis) was measured by an elemental analyser (vario MACRO), while the higher heating value (HHV) was measured based on DIN 51900 standard using a bomb calorimeter (IKA C2000 basic). The com- pound composition of the bio-oil was analysed using a gas chro- matography (Agilent HP 6890) coupled with a mass spectrometer equipped with a capillary column (HP-5 MS). The detected spec- trum was compared with NIST08 mass spectral library for com- pound identification. The GC-MS chromatogram peak area (%) was used as a qualitative representation for the compounds. Gaseous products were analysed using a gas chromatography (Agilent GC 7890B) coupled with a thermal conductivity detector (GC-TCD). Two different columns (Hayesep Q 80/100, Molsieve 5A 60/80) were used to detect gases such as H2, CO, CO2 and CH4.
Fig. 1. Microwave reactor setup for the pyrolysis of horse manure.

11: <header>2.3. Experimental design using full factorial design </header>

Temperature, carrier gas flow rate and microwave receptor proportion pose significant influence on the pyrolysis yield. Therefore, a full factorial design (FFD) was selected to analyse the effects of individual parameter and co-factor interaction as FFD takes into account the combination of levels for all factors tested [27]. Table 1 presents the three factors (temperature, AC/HM ratio and N2 flow rate) and the respective level used. The number of experiments in FFD is given by N = k, where k is the number of factors and r is the number of levels. A total of 27 experimental runs were conducted in this experiment. This setup was to identify the end-product yield distribution by varying the temperature, acti- vated carbon to horse manure (AC/HM) ratio and carrier gas flow rate, or any combination of these factors. The experimental runs were performed in a randomised order as suggested by the run order to mitigate any bias and nuisance variable affecting the py- rolysis process.

The statistical results were subsequently evaluated using anal- ysis of variance (ANOVA) with Minitab software to identify signif- icant factors that influence the yield with a 95% confidence level (p- value < 0.05) [28]. For the lack-of-fit test, the p-value should be insignificant (p-value > 0.05) for the model fitted as it measures

noise-to-signal ratio and is desired to have a value greater than 4. The R-square, Adj R-square and Pred R-square values were also analysed for the optimisation parameters. The response effects based on the interactions of factors were then presented in a con- tour plot. A first order regression model was used to fit the experimental data and determine the coefficient of equation. Square terms and linear-linear interaction terms were not included as they have been proven to be statistically insignificant. The equation can be described as:

where Y is the response (percentage of biochar, bio-oil or gas yield), ao is the constant regression coefficient, ai is the coefficient for linear effect and Xi is the coded independent parameters. Optimi- sation using statistical model derived was done on all three response variables, which are solid (bio-char), liquid (bio-oil) and gaseous products. Validation of the result suggested by the statis- tical model is conducted by performing actual experiment. The validation experiment was triplicated and the average error recorded is 5.2%.

15: <header>2.4. Life cycle assessment (LCA) of horse manure pyrolysis </header>

The life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology was implemented to evaluate the environmental impacts associated with the microwave-induced pyrolysis of horse manure (MIP-HM) into bio- products. The impacts were evaluated using the data obtained from LCA database, namely Ecoinvent 2.0 and GaBi 6, along with reports from reputable energy-related organisations. The impacts were calculated according to the methodology as specified in ISO 14040

Emission AC/HM 0.5 550C, L/min 2.0, Emission|Emission|0.325 t Emission AC/HM 0.5 550C, L/min 2.0, Emission|Emission|Bio-oil 0.392 t Bio-gas "1.0 t Feeding 1.0 Pyrolysis 1.0t Grid Mechanical Electricity 1.31 kW 1.39 Grid MW Heat Electricity Grid Mechanical Equipment Auxillary Electricity 69 kW"|Quench Cooling|0.135 Bio-char "1.0 t Feeding 1.0 Pyrolysis 1.0t Grid Mechanical Electricity 1.31 kW 1.39 Grid MW Heat Electricity Grid Mechanical Equipment Auxillary Electricity 69 kW"|Cold Water Water Pump Warm Water Mechanical 1.46 kW Grid Electricity|0.091 t Water 0.057 t Coke
Fig. 2. System boundary for LCA of HM pyrolysis and the associated inputs and outputs of the processes.

and ISO 14044. Relevant data such as energy used for pyrolysis and processing capacity of an industrial swine manure pyrolysis plant have been reported [29]. In the present study, the plant was assumed to have a processing capacity of 28,000 tonnes horse manure with 7000 working hours per year. The unit processes in MIP-HM include horse manure transportation, drying, grinding, screening, pyrolysing, quenching of volatile for bio-oil, gaseous product recovery and biochar harvesting. Fresh horse manures transported from equine facilities were assumed to contain 40% of moisture [30], which means 1.43 tonnes of fresh horse manures are needed to produce 1 tonne of dried HM with 15% moisture. The

heat required for pyrolysis was assumed to be generated by a mi- crowave reactor, aided by microwave receptor in activated carbon form. The bio-products yield and properties adopted in the LCA were taken from the optimisation process.

2.4.1. Goal and scope The goal of this LCA study is to identify the environmental im- pacts of the thermal decomposition process of horse manure bio- waste. The LCA study adopts the cradle-to-gate approach, with a system boundary that include the collection of manures, processing of manure via microwave-assisted pyrolysis to produce biochar,
Fig. 3. Product yield of gas, liquid and solid from microwave pyrolysis of horse manure based on the run order proposed by Minitab software.

bio-oil and gaseous products. The detail inputs and outputs of the unit process are shown in Fig. 2. Energy inputs for all processes are assumed to be supplied from electricity grid powered by the Con- naught Bridge Power Station in Klang, Selangor, Malaysia, using natural gas as main fuel source. The following assumptions are made:

<list>1. Fresh horse manures are ready to be transported from equine sites. The loading and labor cost associated with manure collection are assumed to be covered by the equine facility and hence not considered in this study. 
2. The pre-processing route of activated carbon was assumed to be the same as horse manure. 
3. The carbon sequential potential and energy content values of the derived products are evaluated from the properties obtained through the current lab-scale study.</list>

The functional unit defined in this study is 1 tonne of dried HM utilised in the pyrolysis plant. All emissions, energy consumption and operating costs are calculated based on the defined functional unit.

2.4.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI) The proposed pyrolysis plant is located at Taman Perindustrian Bukit Raja Selatan, Klang, Selangor, Malaysia (3.0659° N, 101.4746° E). The plant is situated at the center of Peninsula Malaysia acces- sible to most equine facilities situated in Peninsula Malaysia.

The raw (wet) horse manures are transported by diesel- powered trucks. A 3-tonne truck is used for the equine facilities within a radial distance of 100 km while those farther than 100 km utilises the 10-tonne truck. The respective distances of equine fa- cilities in Peninsula Malaysia to the pyrolysis plant is stated in Supplemental Data A. The inputs for this process unit are raw horse

manure from the equine facility and diesel for trucks, while the outputs are the delivered horse manure to the pyrolysis plant. Emissions from the respective mode of transportation are stated in Supplemental Data B. The land distance (obtained via Google Earth Map) from equine facility to plant is considered by including highway and trunk road routes (trunk road is multiplied by a tortuous factor of 1.31 [31]). The economics for HM transportation in Malaysia are calculated via Equations (2) and (3) [32]. It is to note that the averaged distance and transportation price are used for the present analysis, where

A belt-type dryer (SBDD48600SFL) manufactured by SHINCCI is used to dry the raw (wet) horse manure to <10% of moisture. The power required for the dryer with a capacity of 4 tonne/hr is 432 kW. The emission data for HM drying is taken from the emis- sions of natural gas powered grid electricity [33] and is recorded in Supplemental Data C.

The grinding equipment used to reduce the dried horse manure to size of 35 mesh size (0.5 mm) is manufactured by ALPA Powder Tech. It operates with a maximum rotation speed of 3200 rpm, equipped with a grinding disc of 630 mm radius with a power consumption of 55 kW for a capacity of 4 tonne/hr. The ground feedstock is passed through a Machinex Disc Screener with a power consumption of 5 kW, efficiency of 90% and a maximum
Fig. 4. A comparison of predicted yield obtained using statistical models with exper- imental yield for a) bio-char, b) bio-oil and c) gaseous product.
throughput of 5 tonne/hr. Both grinding and screening utilise the power supplied from grid electricity and the detailed emissions are given in Supplemental Data C.

<list>(iv) Feeding 
A screw feeder supplied by Henan Green Eco-Equipment (model LS250) is used to feed the dried, ground horse manure into the pyrolysis reactor. The screw diameter, pitch and rotational speed are 250 mm, 250 mm and 50 rpm, respectively. The delivery value is 0.33 m³/h and consumes 1.327 kW with a capacity of 4 tonne/hr. The power supplied for screw feeder is from the grid electricity with the emissions tabulated in Supplemental Data C. 
(v) Pyrolysis 
Pyrolysis converts horse manure into solid char, non- condensable gas and bio-oil under non-oxidative environment</list>
Fig. 5. Main effect plots for a) bio-char, b) bio-oil and c) gaseous product under the influence of AC/HM ratio, temperature and gas flow.

with the temperature of 550 C. Electricity and horse manure are considered as inputs and the main (major) outputs are bio-char, bio-oil and gaseous products. The microwave pyrolysis reactor is assumed to be in continuous feed operation with a throughput of 4 tonne/hr. The heat consumption for the reactor is 5 GJ/tonne of HM processed while the ancillary process consumes 68.96 kW of power. The emissions of this process are recorded in Supplemental Data C.

A quenching system manufactured by Envitech is used to circulate cooled water at 9 °C at the condensing channel to retrieve condensable vapor in bio-oil form, while the non-condensable vapor is cooled before collected as gas. The quenching system is assumed to be capable of cooling volatiles from 500 C to 25 C with a power consumption of 1.457 kW and throughput of 4 tonne/hr. The power supplied to the quenching system is from grid
electricity. Details on the emissions are shown in Supplemental Data C.

Fig. 5. Main effect plots for a) bio-char, b) bio-oil and c) gaseous product under the influence of AC/HM ratio, temperature and gas flow.

with the temperature of 550 C. Electricity and horse manure are considered as inputs and the main (major) outputs are bio-char, bio-oil and gaseous products. The microwave pyrolysis reactor is assumed to be in continuous feed operation with a throughput of 4 tonne/hr. The heat consumption for the reactor is 5 GJ/tonne of HM processed while the ancillary process consumes 68.96 kW of power. The emissions of this process are recorded in Supplemental Data C.

A quenching system manufactured by Envitech is used to circulate cooled water at 9 °C at the condensing channel to retrieve condensable vapor in bio-oil form, while the non-condensable vapor is cooled before collected as gas. The quenching system is assumed to be capable of cooling volatiles from 500 C to 25 C with a power consumption of 1.457 kW and throughput of 4 tonne/hr. The power supplied to the quenching system is from grid
electricity. Details on the emissions are shown in Supplemental Data C.
18: <header>2.4. Life cycle assessment (LCA) of horse manure pyrolysis </header>

The life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology was implemented to evaluate the environmental impacts associated with the microwave-induced pyrolysis of horse manure (MIP-HM) into bio- products. The impacts were evaluated using the data obtained from LCA database, namely Ecoinvent 2.0 and GaBi 6, along with reports from reputable energy-related organisations. The impacts were calculated according to the methodology as specified in ISO 14040

Emission AC/HM 0.5 550C, L/min 2.0, Emission|Emission|0.325 t Emission AC/HM 0.5 550C, L/min 2.0, Emission|Emission|Bio-oil 0.392 t Bio-gas "1.0 t Feeding 1.0 Pyrolysis 1.0t Grid Mechanical Electricity 1.31 kW 1.39 Grid MW Heat Electricity Grid Mechanical Equipment Auxillary Electricity 69 kW"|Quench Cooling|0.135 Bio-char "1.0 t Feeding 1.0 Pyrolysis 1.0t Grid Mechanical Electricity 1.31 kW 1.39 Grid MW Heat Electricity Grid Mechanical Equipment Auxillary Electricity 69 kW"|Cold Water Water Pump Warm Water Mechanical 1.46 kW Grid Electricity|0.091 t Water 0.057 t Coke
Fig. 2. System boundary for LCA of HM pyrolysis and the associated inputs and outputs of the processes.

and ISO 14044. Relevant data such as energy used for pyrolysis and processing capacity of an industrial swine manure pyrolysis plant have been reported [29]. In the present study, the plant was assumed to have a processing capacity of 28,000 tonnes horse manure with 7000 working hours per year. The unit processes in MIP-HM include horse manure transportation, drying, grinding, screening, pyrolysing, quenching of volatile for bio-oil, gaseous product recovery and biochar harvesting. Fresh horse manures transported from equine facilities were assumed to contain 40% of moisture [30], which means 1.43 tonnes of fresh horse manures are needed to produce 1 tonne of dried HM with 15% moisture. The

heat required for pyrolysis was assumed to be generated by a mi- crowave reactor, aided by microwave receptor in activated carbon form. The bio-products yield and properties adopted in the LCA were taken from the optimisation process.

2.4.1. Goal and scope The goal of this LCA study is to identify the environmental im- pacts of the thermal decomposition process of horse manure bio- waste. The LCA study adopts the cradle-to-gate approach, with a system boundary that include the collection of manures, processing of manure via microwave-assisted pyrolysis to produce biochar,
Fig. 3. Product yield of gas, liquid and solid from microwave pyrolysis of horse manure based on the run order proposed by Minitab software.

bio-oil and gaseous products. The detail inputs and outputs of the unit process are shown in Fig. 2. Energy inputs for all processes are assumed to be supplied from electricity grid powered by the Con- naught Bridge Power Station in Klang, Selangor, Malaysia, using natural gas as main fuel source. The following assumptions are made:

<list>1. Fresh horse manures are ready to be transported from equine sites. The loading and labor cost associated with manure collection are assumed to be covered by the equine facility and hence not considered in this study. 
2. The pre-processing route of activated carbon was assumed to be the same as horse manure. 
3. The carbon sequential potential and energy content values of the derived products are evaluated from the properties obtained through the current lab-scale study.</list>

The functional unit defined in this study is 1 tonne of dried HM utilised in the pyrolysis plant. All emissions, energy consumption and operating costs are calculated based on the defined functional unit.

2.4.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI) The proposed pyrolysis plant is located at Taman Perindustrian Bukit Raja Selatan, Klang, Selangor, Malaysia (3.0659° N, 101.4746° E). The plant is situated at the center of Peninsula Malaysia acces- sible to most equine facilities situated in Peninsula Malaysia.

The raw (wet) horse manures are transported by diesel- powered trucks. A 3-tonne truck is used for the equine facilities within a radial distance of 100 km while those farther than 100 km utilises the 10-tonne truck. The respective distances of equine fa- cilities in Peninsula Malaysia to the pyrolysis plant is stated in Supplemental Data A. The inputs for this process unit are raw horse

manure from the equine facility and diesel for trucks, while the outputs are the delivered horse manure to the pyrolysis plant. Emissions from the respective mode of transportation are stated in Supplemental Data B. The land distance (obtained via Google Earth Map) from equine facility to plant is considered by including highway and trunk road routes (trunk road is multiplied by a tortuous factor of 1.31 [31]). The economics for HM transportation in Malaysia are calculated via Equations (2) and (3) [32]. It is to note that the averaged distance and transportation price are used for the present analysis, where

A belt-type dryer (SBDD48600SFL) manufactured by SHINCCI is used to dry the raw (wet) horse manure to <10% of moisture. The power required for the dryer with a capacity of 4 tonne/hr is 432 kW. The emission data for HM drying is taken from the emis- sions of natural gas powered grid electricity [33] and is recorded in Supplemental Data C.

The grinding equipment used to reduce the dried horse manure to size of 35 mesh size (0.5 mm) is manufactured by ALPA Powder Tech. It operates with a maximum rotation speed of 3200 rpm, equipped with a grinding disc of 630 mm radius with a power consumption of 55 kW for a capacity of 4 tonne/hr. The ground feedstock is passed through a Machinex Disc Screener with a power consumption of 5 kW, efficiency of 90% and a maximum
Fig. 4. A comparison of predicted yield obtained using statistical models with exper- imental yield for a) bio-char, b) bio-oil and c) gaseous product.
throughput of 5 tonne/hr. Both grinding and screening utilise the power supplied from grid electricity and the detailed emissions are given in Supplemental Data C.

<list>(iv) Feeding 
A screw feeder supplied by Henan Green Eco-Equipment (model LS250) is used to feed the dried, ground horse manure into the pyrolysis reactor. The screw diameter, pitch and rotational speed are 250 mm, 250 mm and 50 rpm, respectively. The delivery value is 0.33 m³/h and consumes 1.327 kW with a capacity of 4 tonne/hr. The power supplied for screw feeder is from the grid electricity with the emissions tabulated in Supplemental Data C.

and ISO 14044. Relevant data such as energy used for pyrolysis and processing capacity of an industrial swine manure pyrolysis plant have been reported [29]. In the present study, the plant was assumed to have a processing capacity of 28,000 tonnes horse manure with 7000 working hours per year. The unit processes in MIP-HM include horse manure transportation, drying, grinding, screening, pyrolysing, quenching of volatile for bio-oil, gaseous product recovery and biochar harvesting. Fresh horse manures transported from equine facilities were assumed to contain 40% of moisture [30], which means 1.43 tonnes of fresh horse manures are needed to produce 1 tonne of dried HM with 15% moisture. The

heat required for pyrolysis was assumed to be generated by a mi- crowave reactor, aided by microwave receptor in activated carbon form. The bio-products yield and properties adopted in the LCA were taken from the optimisation process.

2.4.1. Goal and scope The goal of this LCA study is to identify the environmental im- pacts of the thermal decomposition process of horse manure bio- waste. The LCA study adopts the cradle-to-gate approach, with a system boundary that include the collection of manures, processing of manure via microwave-assisted pyrolysis to produce biochar,
Fig. 3. Product yield of gas, liquid and solid from microwave pyrolysis of horse manure based on the run order proposed by Minitab software.

bio-oil and gaseous products. The detail inputs and outputs of the unit process are shown in Fig. 2. Energy inputs for all processes are assumed to be supplied from electricity grid powered by the Con- naught Bridge Power Station in Klang, Selangor, Malaysia, using natural gas as main fuel source. The following assumptions are made:

<list>1. Fresh horse manures are ready to be transported from equine sites. The loading and labor cost associated with manure collection are assumed to be covered by the equine facility and hence not considered in this study. 
2. The pre-processing route of activated carbon was assumed to be the same as horse manure. 
3. The carbon sequential potential and energy content values of the derived products are evaluated from the properties obtained through the current lab-scale study.</list>

The functional unit defined in this study is 1 tonne of dried HM utilised in the pyrolysis plant. All emissions, energy consumption and operating costs are calculated based on the defined functional unit.

2.4.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI) The proposed pyrolysis plant is located at Taman Perindustrian Bukit Raja Selatan, Klang, Selangor, Malaysia (3.0659° N, 101.4746° E). The plant is situated at the center of Peninsula Malaysia acces- sible to most equine facilities situated in Peninsula Malaysia.

The raw (wet) horse manures are transported by diesel- powered trucks. A 3-tonne truck is used for the equine facilities within a radial distance of 100 km while those farther than 100 km utilises the 10-tonne truck. The respective distances of equine fa- cilities in Peninsula Malaysia to the pyrolysis plant is stated in Supplemental Data A. The inputs for this process unit are raw horse

manure from the equine facility and diesel for trucks, while the outputs are the delivered horse manure to the pyrolysis plant. Emissions from the respective mode of transportation are stated in Supplemental Data B. The land distance (obtained via Google Earth Map) from equine facility to plant is considered by including highway and trunk road routes (trunk road is multiplied by a tortuous factor of 1.31 [31]). The economics for HM transportation in Malaysia are calculated via Equations (2) and (3) [32]. It is to note that the averaged distance and transportation price are used for the present analysis, where

A belt-type dryer (SBDD48600SFL) manufactured by SHINCCI is used to dry the raw (wet) horse manure to <10% of moisture. The power required for the dryer with a capacity of 4 tonne/hr is 432 kW. The emission data for HM drying is taken from the emis- sions of natural gas powered grid electricity [33] and is recorded in Supplemental Data C.

The grinding equipment used to reduce the dried horse manure to size of 35 mesh size (0.5 mm) is manufactured by ALPA Powder Tech. It operates with a maximum rotation speed of 3200 rpm, equipped with a grinding disc of 630 mm radius with a power consumption of 55 kW for a capacity of 4 tonne/hr. The ground feedstock is passed through a Machinex Disc Screener with a power consumption of 5 kW, efficiency of 90% and a maximum
Fig. 4. A comparison of predicted yield obtained using statistical models with exper- imental yield for a) bio-char, b) bio-oil and c) gaseous product.
throughput of 5 tonne/hr. Both grinding and screening utilise the power supplied from grid electricity and the detailed emissions are given in Supplemental Data C.

<list>(iv) Feeding 
A screw feeder supplied by Henan Green Eco-Equipment (model LS250) is used to feed the dried, ground horse manure into the pyrolysis reactor. The screw diameter, pitch and rotational speed are 250 mm, 250 mm and 50 rpm, respectively. The delivery value is 0.33 m³/h and consumes 1.327 kW with a capacity of 4 tonne/hr. The power supplied for screw feeder is from the grid electricity with the emissions tabulated in Supplemental Data C. 
(v) Pyrolysis 
Pyrolysis converts horse manure into solid char, non- condensable gas and bio-oil under non-oxidative environment</list>
Fig. 5. Main effect plots for a) bio-char, b) bio-oil and c) gaseous product under the influence of AC/HM ratio, temperature and gas flow.

with the temperature of 550 C. Electricity and horse manure are considered as inputs and the main (major) outputs are bio-char, bio-oil and gaseous products. The microwave pyrolysis reactor is assumed to be in continuous feed operation with a throughput of 4 tonne/hr. The heat consumption for the reactor is 5 GJ/tonne of HM processed while the ancillary process consumes 68.96 kW of power. The emissions of this process are recorded in Supplemental Data C.

A quenching system manufactured by Envitech is used to circulate cooled water at 9 °C at the condensing channel to retrieve condensable vapor in bio-oil form, while the non-condensable vapor is cooled before collected as gas. The quenching system is assumed to be capable of cooling volatiles from 500 C to 25 C with a power consumption of 1.457 kW and throughput of 4 tonne/hr. The power supplied to the quenching system is from grid
electricity. Details on the emissions are shown in Supplemental Data C.

2.4.1. Goal and scope The goal of this LCA study is to identify the environmental im- pacts of the thermal decomposition process of horse manure bio- waste. The LCA study adopts the cradle-to-gate approach, with a system boundary that include the collection of manures, processing of manure via microwave-assisted pyrolysis to produce biochar,
Fig. 3. Product yield of gas, liquid and solid from microwave pyrolysis of horse manure based on the run order proposed by Minitab software.

bio-oil and gaseous products. The detail inputs and outputs of the unit process are shown in Fig. 2. Energy inputs for all processes are assumed to be supplied from electricity grid powered by the Con- naught Bridge Power Station in Klang, Selangor, Malaysia, using natural gas as main fuel source. The following assumptions are made:

<list>1. Fresh horse manures are ready to be transported from equine sites. The loading and labor cost associated with manure collection are assumed to be covered by the equine facility and hence not considered in this study. 
2. The pre-processing route of activated carbon was assumed to be the same as horse manure. 
3. The carbon sequential potential and energy content values of the derived products are evaluated from the properties obtained through the current lab-scale study.</list>

The functional unit defined in this study is 1 tonne of dried HM utilised in the pyrolysis plant. All emissions, energy consumption and operating costs are calculated based on the defined functional unit.

2.4.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI) The proposed pyrolysis plant is located at Taman Perindustrian Bukit Raja Selatan, Klang, Selangor, Malaysia (3.0659° N, 101.4746° E). The plant is situated at the center of Peninsula Malaysia acces- sible to most equine facilities situated in Peninsula Malaysia.

The raw (wet) horse manures are transported by diesel- powered trucks. A 3-tonne truck is used for the equine facilities within a radial distance of 100 km while those farther than 100 km utilises the 10-tonne truck. The respective distances of equine fa- cilities in Peninsula Malaysia to the pyrolysis plant is stated in Supplemental Data A. The inputs for this process unit are raw horse

manure from the equine facility and diesel for trucks, while the outputs are the delivered horse manure to the pyrolysis plant. Emissions from the respective mode of transportation are stated in Supplemental Data B. The land distance (obtained via Google Earth Map) from equine facility to plant is considered by including highway and trunk road routes (trunk road is multiplied by a tortuous factor of 1.31 [31]). The economics for HM transportation in Malaysia are calculated via Equations (2) and (3) [32]. It is to note that the averaged distance and transportation price are used for the present analysis, where

A belt-type dryer (SBDD48600SFL) manufactured by SHINCCI is used to dry the raw (wet) horse manure to <10% of moisture. The power required for the dryer with a capacity of 4 tonne/hr is 432 kW. The emission data for HM drying is taken from the emis- sions of natural gas powered grid electricity [33] and is recorded in Supplemental Data C.

The grinding equipment used to reduce the dried horse manure to size of 35 mesh size (0.5 mm) is manufactured by ALPA Powder Tech. It operates with a maximum rotation speed of 3200 rpm, equipped with a grinding disc of 630 mm radius with a power consumption of 55 kW for a capacity of 4 tonne/hr. The ground feedstock is passed through a Machinex Disc Screener with a power consumption of 5 kW, efficiency of 90% and a maximum
Fig. 4. A comparison of predicted yield obtained using statistical models with exper- imental yield for a) bio-char, b) bio-oil and c) gaseous product.
throughput of 5 tonne/hr. Both grinding and screening utilise the power supplied from grid electricity and the detailed emissions are given in Supplemental Data C.

<list>(iv) Feeding 
A screw feeder supplied by Henan Green Eco-Equipment (model LS250) is used to feed the dried, ground horse manure into the pyrolysis reactor. The screw diameter, pitch and rotational speed are 250 mm, 250 mm and 50 rpm, respectively. The delivery value is 0.33 m³/h and consumes 1.327 kW with a capacity of 4 tonne/hr. The power supplied for screw feeder is from the grid electricity with the emissions tabulated in Supplemental Data C. 
(v) Pyrolysis 
Pyrolysis converts horse manure into solid char, non- condensable gas and bio-oil under non-oxidative environment</list>
Fig. 5. Main effect plots for a) bio-char, b) bio-oil and c) gaseous product under the influence of AC/HM ratio, temperature and gas flow.

with the temperature of 550 C. Electricity and horse manure are considered as inputs and the main (major) outputs are bio-char, bio-oil and gaseous products. The microwave pyrolysis reactor is assumed to be in continuous feed operation with a throughput of 4 tonne/hr. The heat consumption for the reactor is 5 GJ/tonne of HM processed while the ancillary process consumes 68.96 kW of power. The emissions of this process are recorded in Supplemental Data C.

A quenching system manufactured by Envitech is used to circulate cooled water at 9 °C at the condensing channel to retrieve condensable vapor in bio-oil form, while the non-condensable vapor is cooled before collected as gas. The quenching system is assumed to be capable of cooling volatiles from 500 C to 25 C with a power consumption of 1.457 kW and throughput of 4 tonne/hr. The power supplied to the quenching system is from grid
electricity. Details on the emissions are shown in Supplemental Data C.
27: The impact categories considered in this study are 1) global warming potential (GWP), 2) photo-oxidant formation potential (POCP), 3) acidification potential (AP), 4) human toxicity potential (HTP) and 5) eutrophication potential (EP). The impact categories and characterisation factors [34,35] are summarised in Supple- mental Data D.
28: Sensitivity analysis is an essential component in LCA as it ana- lyses the robustness of the results and their sensitivity to un- certainties. In this study, data uncertainty was assessed and quantified for each unit processes. Sensitivity analysis was con- ducted by changing the variant of the operating parameters involved. The feedstock moisture is varied based on the possibility of sun dried (low moisture) or rain soaked (high moisture) condi- tions [36]. For the grinding, feeding and screening operations, each process is integrated with specific equipment. Hence, various equipment manufactured by different companies with the same operation functionality were evaluated and the power consump- tion for each process was compared. For the pyrolysis process, a variant of +15% in electricity consumption was used as suggested by Wang et al. [23]. For the quenching system, power consumed to acquire bio-oil and gas is based on the amount of energy required to

experimental levels of temperature, AC/HM ratio and N2 flow rate as presented in Fig. 3. Detailed characterisation of the product properties are shown in Mong et al. [10]. In the statistical analysis, the main products of bio-oil, biochar and gaseous products are analysed, while the minor products of water and coke are excluded.

The experimental results analysed via ANOVA is shown in Supplemental Data E. From the effects of all three factors, AC/HM ratio is observed to impose higher impact on bio-char and bio-oil yields, whereas temperature is shown to have stronger effect on gas yield due to higher F-value. On the other hand, N2 flow rate has the weakest effect among all three factors. It is observed that all factors are statistically significant. In addition, the degree of freedom (DF) indicates the level of predictive capability, implying high confidence level in the model formed. The lowest DF attained among the three models generated is 19, proving that the all models are 'unsaturated' with sufficient error DF and data. These models are capable of estimating the variance and producing a predictive statistical model.

Linear regression was used to statistically analyse the output response of solid yield. The three main factors (temperature, AC/ HM ratio and N2 flow) were found to be statistically significant (P- value < 0.05) for solid yield. One high residual data is detected while the predictive capability of R-sq(adj) and R-sq(pred) are 86.81% and 83.59%, respectively, which corresponds to high pre- dictive level of the equation:
Biochar (wt%) = 117.33 - 18.7 AC / HM Ratio + 11. X Gas Flow min) - 0.1325 X Temperature (C)

cool the hot vapor to room temperature. The volatiles that are cooled during transfer before condensation are assumed to be within the temperature range of 325-520 °C. Finally, the findings in this work are compared with existing animal manure pyrolysis facilities and other horse manure management methods. The car- bon sequential and energy content of the pyrolytic products are calculated.

For the liquid phase product, the three main factors were also found to be significant at high confidence level (P-value < 0.05) as shown in Supplemental Data E. High residual data is not detected in this model, hence, the higher predictive capability of R-sq(adj) and R-sq(pred) are 89.37% and 86.48%, respectively, when compared with the solid-model. The regression equation is given as follow:
Bio - oil (wt%) = 0.31 + AC / HM Ratio - 3.43 X Gas Flow min) + 0.03324 X Temperature(°C)

In the statistical model for gaseous product, a large residue is observed. The predictive capability of R-sq(adj) and R-sq(pred) are 80.00% and 76.12%, respectively, with the regression equation of:

The lower value of model predictability of gaseous product is due to the measurement of gaseous yield, which is obtained through mass balance equation. It is noted that a low R-sq(pred) value indicates the unsuitability of equation for quantitative pre- diction but is more preferred for qualitative measurement. How- ever, in the current study, all three regression models achieved an R-sq(pred) > 75%, indicating high confidence level of using this statistical model for quantitative prediction. Besides, the variance inflation factor (VIF) in all the three models have a maximum value of 1.03 (close to 1), which clearly indicates the three input factors are a set of independent variables. This eliminates the problem of co-linearity that might cloud the individual effect of other pre- dictors, leading to inaccuracy in the prediction. Fig. 4 shows the experimental and predicted yields of bio-char, bio-oil and gaseous

product using regression model of Equations (4)-(6). The predicted yields for all three end products are reported to only deviate slightly from the experimental yield and is deemed to be satisfac- tory. The coefficient of correlation (R2) for yields of bio-char, bio-oil and bio-gas are 0.886, 0.898 and 0.823, respectively.

Fig. 5 shows the main effect of the factors on the yield of bio- char, bio-oil and gas. From the factorial plot, each factor imposes a unique variation on the effect of end product yields. For instance, AC/HM ratio and temperature are found to be inversely propor- tional to the bio-char yield but directly proportional to the bio-oil and gas yield. This observation is in agreement with most biomass pyrolysis studies, where higher energy supplied to the system promotes devolatilisation process and reduces final leftover (bio-char) [18,37]. On the contrary, N2 flow rate imposes an
Fig. 6. The yields of biogas (a,b,c), bio-oil (d,e,f) and bio-char (g,h,i) derived from the microwave pyrolysis of horse manure under different operating parameters.

opposite trend when compared with the temperature and AC/HM ratio. An increase in carrier gas flow increases the bio-char yield and lowers the bio-oil and gas yield. From the gradient curve of each factorial plot, the effect of AC/HM is more prominent in the range of 1.0-2.0 in comparison with low AC concentration feed- stock. Activation carbon acts as microwave absorber, hence higher AC portion promotes rapid heating and devolatilisation process which greatly reduces bio-char yield whilst increases both bio-oil and gaseous product yields. The effect of temperature between the range of 350-450° and 450-550° is almost similar in which a linear relationship is portrayed. The N2 flow rate is observed to be least impactful on the bio-oil yield. The gas yield derived at 1.0-1.5 L/min of N2 flow seems almost insignificant due to high carrier gas flow rate, where the cooling effect of carrier gas on the evolved volatiles is more prominent than removing volatiles from the reaction zone. Subsequently, the cooled volatiles condense on the bio-char surface, blocking subsequent volatiles from evapo- rating [38]. This could also induce a drop in bio-oil yield and lead to an increase in bio-char yield.

The significance of the process parameters obtained in this statistical analysis can be explained through the nature of pyrolysis process. Since pyrolysis is a thermally induced process, catalyst ratio and pyrolysis temperature are thus found to be of higher significance. The flow rate of the carrier gas involves fluid dy- namics, which is a physical phenomenon that is not as important as those of thermal properties. Activated carbon plays a major role in absorbing and converting microwaves into heat, which subse- quently leads to the decomposition of feedstock. Therefore, the importance of AC content is observed to be affecting the yield distribution at a higher level as compared to N2 flow.

The effects of temperature, AC/HM ratio and N2 gas flow on the production of bio-char, bio-oil and gaseous products are shown in the contour plots in Fig. 6. Fig. 6a-f show the relationship of all three factors with gaseous and liquid products. Temperature and AC/HM ratio are proportional to the yield of bio-oil and gas yield (Fig. 6b and e). This is due to the fact that an increase in any of the two factors will impose higher heating rate during pyrolysis, leading to a higher decomposition rate with more volatiles (bio-oil and gas) yielded. When the N2 carrier flow is increased, a drop in gaseous yield (Fig. 6a and c) is observed due to lower residence time available for secondary decomposition of volatile. Bio-oil yield is found to be highest at moderate L/min) N2 flow (Fig. 6d and e), suggesting a possible trade-off effect at high and low N2 flow. A high gas flow possibly imposes cooling effect on evolved 'hot' volatile that could cause recondensation, resulting in lower liquid yield. A low gas flow prolongs volatile residence time, which en- ables secondary cracking of volatiles into light molecular gas, hence contributing to lower liquid yield. Similar situation has been re- ported by Ref. [21] in the pyrolysis of oil palm shell waste, of which reduced bio-oil yield was observed with high carrier gas flow rate.

The factors of temperature and AC/HM ratio are found to vary inversely with biochar yield (Fig. 6h). The pyrolysis temperature factor reduces biochar yield as they promote decomposition of horse manure. On the other hand, biochar yield is found to increase with N2 flow (Fig. 6g and i), owing to the condensation effect induced by the incoming carrier gas at room temperature. The contact of carrier gas and freshly evolved 'hot' volatile experiences 'cooling', leading to the recondensation of vapor on the biochar surface. This could then clog the pores on the biochar surface and prevents subsequent volatiles from evolving. As a result, the bio- char yield is increased.

opposite trend when compared with the temperature and AC/HM ratio. An increase in carrier gas flow increases the bio-char yield and lowers the bio-oil and gas yield. From the gradient curve of each factorial plot, the effect of AC/HM is more prominent in the range of 1.0-2.0 in comparison with low AC concentration feed- stock. Activation carbon acts as microwave absorber, hence higher AC portion promotes rapid heating and devolatilisation process which greatly reduces bio-char yield whilst increases both bio-oil and gaseous product yields. The effect of temperature between the range of 350-450° and 450-550° is almost similar in which a linear relationship is portrayed. The N2 flow rate is observed to be least impactful on the bio-oil yield. The gas yield derived at 1.0-1.5 L/min of N2 flow seems almost insignificant due to high carrier gas flow rate, where the cooling effect of carrier gas on the evolved volatiles is more prominent than removing volatiles from the reaction zone. Subsequently, the cooled volatiles condense on the bio-char surface, blocking subsequent volatiles from evapo- rating [38]. This could also induce a drop in bio-oil yield and lead to an increase in bio-char yield.

The significance of the process parameters obtained in this statistical analysis can be explained through the nature of pyrolysis process. Since pyrolysis is a thermally induced process, catalyst ratio and pyrolysis temperature are thus found to be of higher significance. The flow rate of the carrier gas involves fluid dy- namics, which is a physical phenomenon that is not as important as those of thermal properties. Activated carbon plays a major role in absorbing and converting microwaves into heat, which subse- quently leads to the decomposition of feedstock. Therefore, the importance of AC content is observed to be affecting the yield distribution at a higher level as compared to N2 flow.

The effects of temperature, AC/HM ratio and N2 gas flow on the production of bio-char, bio-oil and gaseous products are shown in the contour plots in Fig. 6. Fig. 6a-f show the relationship of all three factors with gaseous and liquid products. Temperature and AC/HM ratio are proportional to the yield of bio-oil and gas yield (Fig. 6b and e). This is due to the fact that an increase in any of the two factors will impose higher heating rate during pyrolysis, leading to a higher decomposition rate with more volatiles (bio-oil and gas) yielded. When the N2 carrier flow is increased, a drop in gaseous yield (Fig. 6a and c) is observed due to lower residence time available for secondary decomposition of volatile. Bio-oil yield is found to be highest at moderate L/min) N2 flow (Fig. 6d and e), suggesting a possible trade-off effect at high and low N2 flow. A high gas flow possibly imposes cooling effect on evolved 'hot' volatile that could cause recondensation, resulting in lower liquid yield. A low gas flow prolongs volatile residence time, which en- ables secondary cracking of volatiles into light molecular gas, hence contributing to lower liquid yield. Similar situation has been re- ported by Ref. [21] in the pyrolysis of oil palm shell waste, of which reduced bio-oil yield was observed with high carrier gas flow rate.

The factors of temperature and AC/HM ratio are found to vary inversely with biochar yield (Fig. 6h). The pyrolysis temperature factor reduces biochar yield as they promote decomposition of horse manure. On the other hand, biochar yield is found to increase with N2 flow (Fig. 6g and i), owing to the condensation effect induced by the incoming carrier gas at room temperature. The contact of carrier gas and freshly evolved 'hot' volatile experiences 'cooling', leading to the recondensation of vapor on the biochar surface. This could then clog the pores on the biochar surface and prevents subsequent volatiles from evolving. As a result, the bio- char yield is increased.

In the statistical model for gaseous product, a large residue is observed. The predictive capability of R-sq(adj) and R-sq(pred) are 80.00% and 76.12%, respectively, with the regression equation of:

The lower value of model predictability of gaseous product is due to the measurement of gaseous yield, which is obtained through mass balance equation. It is noted that a low R-sq(pred) value indicates the unsuitability of equation for quantitative pre- diction but is more preferred for qualitative measurement. How- ever, in the current study, all three regression models achieved an R-sq(pred) > 75%, indicating high confidence level of using this statistical model for quantitative prediction. Besides, the variance inflation factor (VIF) in all the three models have a maximum value of 1.03 (close to 1), which clearly indicates the three input factors are a set of independent variables. This eliminates the problem of co-linearity that might cloud the individual effect of other pre- dictors, leading to inaccuracy in the prediction. Fig. 4 shows the experimental and predicted yields of bio-char, bio-oil and gaseous

product using regression model of Equations (4)-(6). The predicted yields for all three end products are reported to only deviate slightly from the experimental yield and is deemed to be satisfac- tory. The coefficient of correlation (R2) for yields of bio-char, bio-oil and bio-gas are 0.886, 0.898 and 0.823, respectively.

The factors of temperature and AC/HM ratio are found to vary inversely with biochar yield (Fig. 6h). The pyrolysis temperature factor reduces biochar yield as they promote decomposition of horse manure. On the other hand, biochar yield is found to increase with N2 flow (Fig. 6g and i), owing to the condensation effect induced by the incoming carrier gas at room temperature. The contact of carrier gas and freshly evolved 'hot' volatile experiences 'cooling', leading to the recondensation of vapor on the biochar surface. This could then clog the pores on the biochar surface and prevents subsequent volatiles from evolving. As a result, the bio- char yield is increased.
41: Optimisation on pyrolysis factors is conducted to determine the optimal condition for maximum yield of bio-oil and gaseous

product whilst minimising the yield of bio-char. As observed in Fig. 3, the cases with the highest yield of bio-oil and gaseous product are derived from different run orders. Therefore, optimi- sation for all three responses is conducted through a linear regression model. The criteria for all factors are set in ranges with the final goal for all three responses. The aim of this optimisation study is to produce good quality end products with high yield.

All responses were given equal importance during optimisation process as pyrolysis is able to yield all three end products. The criteria for all factors were in the range with maximum goals for gaseous product and bio-oil and minimum goal for bio-char. Three solutions were generated through the statistically optimised pro- cess (Supplemental Data F). The highest desirability (0.9697) is achieved for AC/HM ratio of 2.0, N2 flow rate of 0.5 L/min and temperature of 550 C to attain gaseous product, bio-oil and bio- char yield of 41.2 wt%, 34.9 wt% and 12.6 wt% respectively. A desirability close to 1 indicates a good parametric condition to optimise the required responses [39]. Further experiments were then carried out based on the predicted conditions in triplicates, yielding bio-char, bio-oil and bio-gas of 13.5 wt%, 32.5 wt% and 39.2 wt%, respectively. An average deviation of +5.2% is recorded in comparison of predicted yield (theoretical) and actual yield (experimental), indicating good agreement between the two.

43: A large surface area on the biochar (799.57 was detected, which indicates the potential of bio-char as bio-adsorbent. The bio- char surface morphology and element composition were examined using a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (Hitachi-SU8020) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (X-MAXN269), respectively. The image of biochar surface shown in Fig. 7a was taken under a magnification 2k. There are numerous pores (suitable as adsorption site) formed on the bio-char surface as a result of devolatilisation during pyrolysis. The release of volatiles and oxygenated compound formed carbon dense solid product. The high carbon composition (77.9 within the bio-char structure is illustrated in the spectrum of EDX analysis as shown in Fig. 7b. Besides, potassium, sodium and magnesium that originate from the raw feedstock are also present in the biochar, indicating its po- tential for soil amendment. The biochar attained a heating value of 24.19 MJ/kg which corresponds to a rise of 38.7% in energy density when compared with the initial heating value of 17.44 MJ/kg. The produced bio-char with high energy density can be used as solid fuel.
44: The bio-oil obtained is light brown in colour and acidic in nature with a pH value of 3.99-4.00. The main elements of bio-oil are 41.82%carbon, 5.38% hydrogen, 14.3% nitrogen, 1.05% sulphur and 37.45% oxygen. The calorific value (HHV) of bio-oil is 18.14 MJ/kg, which is comparatively lower compared to conventional diesel fuel (42 MJ/kg) due to its high oxygen content. The compound composition of the bio-oil consists of highly oxygenated com- pounds, corroborating with the result of high oxygen content detected through the elemental analysis. Phenols compound is found to be the highest (68.67%), followed by ketones (14.20%) and pyridine (~13.37%) while carboxylic acids formed the smallest portion of 3.76%. The chemical compounds detected in bio-oil through GC-MS is provided in Supplemental Data G. The high concentration of phenols within bio-oil suggests its potential as bio-chemical via phenol extraction. The bio-phenol can be a replacement to fossil-based phenol for the production of renewable resin. Ketones can also be extracted from the bio-oil for the

46: Within the gaseous product composition, a major portion of syngas (67.17 vol%) consisting of 14.98 vol% of H2 and 52.18 vol% of CO was detected. The major CO portion within the biogas yield

might be due to the dominance of the Boudouard reaction, where solid C reacts with CO2 to form CO. At high temperature, the solid- gas interaction is intense due to sufficient heat supplied to over- come energy barrier. The CH4 produced was recorded at 11.21 vol% while CO2 made up 21.62 vol% of the gaseous product. Methanation reaction, where water formed during pyrolysis, is believed to react with solid C in forming CH4 and CO2. The LHV of gas is 12.23 MJ/m³, which is comparable to various other pyrolysis-derived gas from biomass sources [22,41]. Although the heating value of the gaseous product is lower as compared to natural gas (~38 MJ/m³), the main advantage of the biogas is its carbon neutrality. Thus, the biogas can be used as supplemental gaseous fuel for heat and power genera- tion to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
47: 3.3.1. Energy consumption and economics of various unit processes For the present life cycle assessment, the energy consumption of each unit process is shown in Fig. 8. An estimated total of 568.27 kWh of energy is consumed during the processing of 1 tonne dried HM. The heat required for the entire pyrolysis process route is 520.14 kWh/tonne (91.55% of the total energy), which is used by the heating and drying processes. The other processes powered by electricity have been observed to contribute to less than a tenth of the energy consumed.

The price required for processing 1 tonne of dried HM is shown Fig. 9. The price is calculated based on the grid electricity charges for medium voltage industries under category E1 supplied by the Malaysian utility company, Tenaga Nasional Berhad [42]. The transportation prices are calculated using Eq. (2) and Eq. (3) for biomass transportation in Peninsular Malaysia [32]. From the calculation, transportation is observed to require the highest cost, followed by the electricity price for pyrolysis and drying. As all equine facilities are scattered throughout Peninsula Malaysia with the distance from the pyrolysis facility ranging from 12.7 to 367 km, the cost of collecting raw HM is higher for farther location as more trucks are required. In addition, the offset of cost also comes in the form of tipping fees - RM148 for one tonne of raw HM is paid by equine facilities. This is in line with the solid waste management landfill tipping fee in Malaysia [43]. It is assumed that for the purpose of this economic analysis, the HM collection replaces the cost required to transport them for landfill. It has been reported that significant amount of HM are sent to landfill while the rest are being used for soil amendment [44]. It is to note that the equine
Fig. 10. Environmental impacts of various unit processes involved in the microwave pyrolysis of 1-tonne of dried HM.

facilities in Malaysia are not linked to farms, hence, it is assumed that all HM are sent to landfill instead of being used as organic fertiliser. This would sum up to a net processing cost of RM129.22/ tonne HM.

50: The potential environmental impacts of each unit process for the microwave pyrolysis of HM is shown in Fig. 10 while details of the breakdown are shown in Supplemental Data H. The GWP impact of microwave pyrolysis of 1 tonne of dried HM is evaluated from the emissions of CO2, CH4, NOx and N20. The total GWP is computed in terms of CO2 equivalent (kg CO2 eq). A total of 228.6 kg CO2 eq of greenhouse gas is emitted per tonne of dried HM pro- cessed. The greenhouse gases are mainly originated from the heating during pyrolysis, which accounts for 56.8% of total emis- sion. The drying of raw HM contributes to 28.2% of GWP. As all unit processes operate on electric supply, the most energy intensive process consumes the most electricity, thus contributing the highest level of GWP. Transportation, on the other hand, contrib- utes approximately 7.18% of the total GWP.

The acidification potential (AP) is related to the emission of acidic products, e.g. SO2, NOx, NH3 and hydrocarbons that have significant impact on the environment. These acidic substances can interact with others atmospheric elements to produce acid rain. The acid rain could reduce soil and water pH and affect the habitat of living organisms. The impact of acidification potential is typically expressed in equivalent SO2 emission (SO2 eq). A total of 105 g of SO2 eq are released during the microwave pyrolysis of HM (per tonne basis). Fig. 10 shows that transportation is the largest contributor to acidification emission (54.9%), followed by the electricity usage for heat supplied during pyrolysis (27.6%) and drying (13.7%). According to the inventory in Supplemental Data B and C, the land transportation emits 0.453 g of SO2 for every km travel, whereas the grid electricity releases 0.002 g of SO2 for every kWh generated. This is due to the nature of power source in which the land transportation consumes diesel (Euro II), which generally produces higher sulphur content as compared to natural gas burned in power plant [45].

Eutrophication potential (EP) is defined as the increase in rate of supply of organic matter in the ecosystem. It is evaluated based on the content of NH3 and NOx and expressed as PO4 equivalent (PO4 eq). The EP evaluates all impacts from the excessive level of macronutrients in the environment emitted to air, water and soil. From the present analysis, a total of 23.9 g PO43 eq is expected to be emitted from the cradle-to-gate process of HM. Transportation contributes the greatest amount of EP (53.2%), followed by the heat

required for pyrolysis (28.7%) and the drying process (14.2%). From the inventory in Supplemental Data B, the NO emission of 6.02 g for every km travel using diesel powered truck is significantly higher than the emissions from grid electric supply (0.09 g of NO and 0.02 g of NH3 for every kWh generated). Similar to the LCA study of MSW using fast pyrolysis conducted by Wang et al. [23], the highest EP reported is also from diesel vehicle operation (55.6%).

Human toxicity potential (HTP) refers to the potential impact on human health when exposed to harmful products including car- cinogens and non-carcinogens. The Dichlorobenzene (DCB) equiv- alent is used to measure the magnitude of HTP, which is evaluated from the emissions of dust, particulate matter, NOx, NH3 and SO2. A total of 214 g DCB eq is recorded (shown in Supplemental Data H) for processing 1 tonne of dried HM. Transportation is attributed as the main source of HTP (55.7%), which is mostly due to the par- ticulate matter emissions from vehicle exhaust (0.101 g of PM for every km travel). To reduce the HTP, the trucks used should be installed with particulate matter or dust filter to reduce the amount of particulates released to air.

Photo-oxidant formation potential (POCP) is a metric used to evaluate the effect of 'photo-oxidant formation' from volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in the atmosphere. Ozone can be formed from the reaction of VOCs and NOx with sunlight. The POCP is evaluated by ethane equivalent, C2H6 eq. From the Supplemental Data H, 7.21 g of C2H6 eq is expected to be emitted from pyrolysing 1 tonne of HM, where the transportation contributes the highest portion (60.9%), followed by the electric supplied for pyrolysis (23.9%) and the drying process (11.9%).

The greenhouse gases are mainly originated from the heating during pyrolysis, which accounts for 56.8% of total emis- sion. The drying of raw HM contributes to 28.2% of GWP. As all unit processes operate on electric supply, the most energy intensive process consumes the most electricity, thus contributing the highest level of GWP. Transportation, on the other hand, contrib- utes approximately 7.18% of the total GWP.

The acidification potential (AP) is related to the emission of acidic products, e.g. SO2, NOx, NH3 and hydrocarbons that have significant impact on the environment. These acidic substances can interact with others atmospheric elements to produce acid rain. The acid rain could reduce soil and water pH and affect the habitat of living organisms. The impact of acidification potential is typically expressed in equivalent SO2 emission (SO2 eq). A total of 105 g of SO2 eq are released during the microwave pyrolysis of HM (per tonne basis). Fig. 10 shows that transportation is the largest contributor to acidification emission (54.9%), followed by the electricity usage for heat supplied during pyrolysis (27.6%) and drying (13.7%). According to the inventory in Supplemental Data B and C, the land transportation emits 0.453 g of SO2 for every km travel, whereas the grid electricity releases 0.002 g of SO2 for every kWh generated. This is due to the nature of power source in which the land transportation consumes diesel (Euro II), which generally produces higher sulphur content as compared to natural gas burned in power plant [45].

Eutrophication potential (EP) is defined as the increase in rate of supply of organic matter in the ecosystem. It is evaluated based on the content of NH3 and NOx and expressed as PO4 equivalent (PO4 eq). The EP evaluates all impacts from the excessive level of macronutrients in the environment emitted to air, water and soil. From the present analysis, a total of 23.9 g PO43 eq is expected to be emitted from the cradle-to-gate process of HM. Transportation contributes the greatest amount of EP (53.2%), followed by the heat

required for pyrolysis (28.7%) and the drying process (14.2%). From the inventory in Supplemental Data B, the NO emission of 6.02 g for every km travel using diesel powered truck is significantly higher than the emissions from grid electric supply (0.09 g of NO and 0.02 g of NH3 for every kWh generated). Similar to the LCA study of MSW using fast pyrolysis conducted by Wang et al. [23], the highest EP reported is also from diesel vehicle operation (55.6%).

Human toxicity potential (HTP) refers to the potential impact on human health when exposed to harmful products including car- cinogens and non-carcinogens. The Dichlorobenzene (DCB) equiv- alent is used to measure the magnitude of HTP, which is evaluated from the emissions of dust, particulate matter, NOx, NH3 and SO2. A total of 214 g DCB eq is recorded (shown in Supplemental Data H) for processing 1 tonne of dried HM. Transportation is attributed as the main source of HTP (55.7%), which is mostly due to the par- ticulate matter emissions from vehicle exhaust (0.101 g of PM for every km travel). To reduce the HTP, the trucks used should be installed with particulate matter or dust filter to reduce the amount of particulates released to air.

Photo-oxidant formation potential (POCP) is a metric used to evaluate the effect of 'photo-oxidant formation' from volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in the atmosphere. Ozone can be formed from the reaction of VOCs and NOx with sunlight. The POCP is evaluated by ethane equivalent, C2H6 eq. From the Supplemental Data H, 7.21 g of C2H6 eq is expected to be emitted from pyrolysing 1 tonne of HM, where the transportation contributes the highest portion (60.9%), followed by the electric supplied for pyrolysis (23.9%) and the drying process (11.9%).
55: The sensitivity analysis on GWP for each unit processes in the microwave pyrolysis of 1 tonne dried HM is analysed. The param- eters investigated include feedstock moisture, power required for grinding, screening, feeding, pyrolysis and quenching, as shown in Fig. 11. Overall, the net GWP is found to vary from 164.13 to 267.57 kg CO2 eq per tonne of dried HM. The moisture content in the collected HM imposes the highest variation to the total GWP. When the collected HM are sun-dried to a moisture content of 15% [36], the total GWP would be reduced by 22.65%. However, if the manures are exposed to rain prior to collection, the GWP of the process would then be increased by 26.10%. This significant differ- ence is attributable to the amount of energy required in the drying of HM before the pyrolysis process. The energy consumption of pyrolysis reactor also poses a significant impact on the overall GWP. If the power usage during pyrolysis is increased by 15%, the total

UMC-unmanaged composting, MC-managed composting, LSI-large scale incineration, SSI-small scale incineration and AD-anaerobic digestion from literature [47] and MP- microwave pyrolysis (current study).

GWP will be increased by 9.63%. From the sensitivity analysis, the other unit processes are found to have comparatively lower effects on GWP as compared to the feedstock moisture and the power required for the pyrolysis process.

The drying of HM to remove the moisture in the feedstock is another critical process that affects the GWP significantly. Higher moisture content in feedsstock demands more energy for drying. Located near the equator, Malaysia receives a constant monthly solar irradiance of 400-600 MJ/m2 throughout the year [46], providing the advantage of utilising natural sunlight for HM pre- processing (drying). This would significantly reduce the overall energy consumption along with the net GWP. However, Malaysia also experiences a significant amount of rainfall (2500 mm), in particular during the Northeast (Nov-Mar) and Southwest (May-Sep) monsoon seasons which may affect the availability of sunlight [46] and increase the risk of rain-soaked HM. Therefore, extra effort and cost are needed, such as building shelter to prevent soaking of feedstock due to the rain.

Despite pyrolysis being the main process in the HM processing facility, its impact on GWP comes in second. This shows that the entire microwave pyrolysis process is feedstock dependent rather than process dependent. The type of feedstock with lower moisture content could improve overall process feasibility with an attempt to lower energy consumption and reduce GWP. The auxiliary pro- cesses such as grinding, screening, feeding and quenching

59: <header>3.3.4. Comparison with other waste management methods </header>

The environmental impacts of microwave pyrolysis on HM are compared with other HM management methods such as com- posting, incineration and anaerobic digestion, as shown in Table 2. The values in Table 2 are derived solely based on the unit processes involoved, without taking into account the potential impact of the end product. For example, the energy attained by the end product of HM is not considered in the energy usage. Hence, it is expected that the microwave pyrolysis is more energy-intenstive, which leads to higher GWP per tonne basis. Composting and anaerobic digestion both have comparable energy usage because external heat is also required to keep the feedstock at certain temperature for degradation to occur. Incineration generally consumes lower energy as the combustion process is self-sustaining as HM contains energy value. The main portion of GWP for microwave pyrolysis emanates from the transportation of HM and energy used in each of the unit process.

In spite of the intensitve energy consumption, microwave py- rolysis presents the advantage of producing end products with good energy value. Table 3 shows the bio-products obtained from this research work with their energy content and carbon storage potential. The bio-char derived from this work can be used as fuel, adsorbent and fertilisers. In Malaysia, the common usage of bio- char are for energy production (28%) and soil amendment (car- bon sink) (72%) [48]. Bio-oil derived can be upgraded into bio-fuel or extracted to produce bio-chemicals. Since the bio-oil contains a heating value of 18.14 MJ/kg, it is more profitable to derive bio- chemicals from the bio-oil due to its high phenolic content instead of using it as a relatively low calorific fuel. The utilisation of bio-oil in the chemical industries is also expected to contribute to carbon storage. On the other hand, the gaseous product with high level of CO can also be used as gaseous fuel for heat and power

61: The AP and EP of microwave pyrolysis are generally lower as compared to other managing processes. Both the large and small- scale incineration methods release acidic gases during combus- tion as diesel fuel is required for the start up and maintaining the temperature of the combined heat and power plant. The anaerobic digestion produces secondary sludge that requires treatment or to be landfilled, so is the incineration process that produces ash and slag. These processes contribute to the impact categories of AP and EP. Although microwave pyrolysis requires higher amount of en- ergy and generates a higher GWP as compared to the conventional managing methods, the valuable products derived can offset these disadvantages by having bio-fuels to generate power and bio- products to act as carbon storage sink.

A conventional pyrolysis facility processing swine manure in Belgium with an annual operating hours of 7000 [29] is compared to the current work to assess the energy efficiency and environ- mental impacts. The feedstock moisture content and operating conditions of both cases are relatively similar. In Belgium, swine manure with moisture content of 44 wt% is used as feedstock, while the pyrolysis process is conducted in a conventional reactor at 500 °C for 1 h. On the other hand, the present work utilises HM with 40 wt% moisture, while the microwave pyrolysis is conducted at 550 for 1 h. The energy consumption for microwave pyrolysis (568.27 1 is reported to be lower by a factor of 2.5 as compared to conventional pyrolysis (1694.4 kWh), indicating higher energy efficiency for the former. It has been reported that microwave heating is able to reduce energy consumption by more than 50% as compared to conventional heating [49]. On product yield, the swine manure with conventional pyrolysis generates 48.8 wt% of bio-char, 23.3 wt% of bio-oil and 27.9 wt% gas, as opposed to the present microwave pyrolysis of HM that produces 13.5 wt% of bio-char, 32.5 wt% of bio-oil and 39.2 wt% gas. The increased yield in bio- oil and biogas shows the advantage of higher conversion effi- ciency for microwave pyrolysis.

From the perspective of environmental impact, HM microwave pyrolysis plant has lower GWP as compared to swine manure conventional pyrolysis. Although both facilities utilise grid elec- tricity (fuel source - natural gas) for heating purposes in drying and pyrolysis processes, the net emission per unit of energy consumed is expected be comparable. However, the higher energy consumed for swine manure pyrolysis results in higher GWP of 599.7 kg CO2 eq, as compared to the HM microwave pyrolysis with the GHG emission intensity of 228.6 kg CO2 eq. The comparison indicates that the more energy efficient microwave pyrolysis process can lead to lower energy consumption and lower GHG emissions. In short, microwave pyrolysis is found to be a more effective heating method in processing horse manure. Although this process consumes higher energy and GWP as compared to the conventional means of managing horse manure, the bio-product produced can be used for power production and carbon storage sink, while off- setting other negative impacts.

64: From the present study, pyrolysing horse manure with microwave-assisted heating method is found to produce bio-char with improved energy density, and increase bio-oil and gaseous product yields. Among the pyrolysis parameters tested, AC/HM ratio is found to have the highest impact on the end product yield distribution, while N2 carrier gas flow rate has the least effect. The optimisation study conducted shows that pyrolysis temperature of 550 °C, N2 flow of 0.5 L/min and AC/HM ratio of 2.0 produces the highest gaseous (39.2 wt%) and bio-oil (32.5 wt%) yields while maintaining minimum yield of biochar at 13.5 wt%. Using the

optimum pyrolysis case as the basis for life cycle assessment, the pyrolysis of horse manure is simulated in a modelled industrial plant to produce useful end products. The drying and pyrolysis stages are the most energy intensive processes and incur the highest cost. The most carbon-intensive process is attributed to the heat required for the pyrolysis process, which accounts up to 56.8% of the total emitted GHG. On the other hand, the transportation of horse manure produces the largest impact on AP, EP, HTP and POCP, while amounting to about 60% of the total emissions. The study shows that the microwave-induced pyrolysis of horse manure has lower environmental impact as compared to conventional pyrolysis of swine manure due to its lower energy consumption and higher conversion efficiency. Valorisation of the manures via pyrolysis results in the production of three phases of end products which can be utilised as bioenergy and biochemicals, thereby reducing the carbon footprint while concomitantly treating the manures. The LCA study also shows that microwave-assisted pyrolysis is a promising manure treatment method that can contribute to the sustainability goal of GWP reduction.

The optimisation study conducted shows that pyrolysis temperature of 550 °C, N2 flow of 0.5 L/min and AC/HM ratio of 2.0 produces the highest gaseous (39.2 wt%) and bio-oil (32.5 wt%) yields while maintaining minimum yield of biochar at 13.5 wt%. Using the

optimum pyrolysis case as the basis for life cycle assessment, the pyrolysis of horse manure is simulated in a modelled industrial plant to produce useful end products. The drying and pyrolysis stages are the most energy intensive processes and incur the highest cost. The most carbon-intensive process is attributed to the heat required for the pyrolysis process, which accounts up to 56.8% of the total emitted GHG. On the other hand, the transportation of horse manure produces the largest impact on AP, EP, HTP and POCP, while amounting to about 60% of the total emissions. The study shows that the microwave-induced pyrolysis of horse manure has lower environmental impact as compared to conventional pyrolysis of swine manure due to its lower energy consumption and higher conversion efficiency. Valorisation of the manures via pyrolysis results in the production of three phases of end products which can be utilised as bioenergy and biochemicals, thereby reducing the carbon footprint while concomitantly treating the manures. The LCA study also shows that microwave-assisted pyrolysis is a promising manure treatment method that can contribute to the sustainability goal of GWP reduction.
